paragraphs.body: I stood facing the court. This court had floors that were darker than the average wood gym floor. A big blue M was in the middle beneath the net for the high school's logo. The court lines were a very faint yellow that blended too much with the wood. We were down by two sets and had no hope of coming back being down by a couple of points in the third. We lost the first point I was on the bench for by shanking the pass far behind any of the girl's reach. We then had to serve and receive the ball dropped right in front of our passer on the right, diving too late for it. It was game point them now, and they served the ball to her again. She stepped to the side too late, missing the ball, and it dropped on our side of the court. The other team was cheering in a huddle on their side of the court. We were in a huddle crying as our season and my career ended. We were on opposite sides of the net fiving one another under it, saying good game even though we didn't mean it. We then lined up for medals directly across from one of their players. We gave them their gold medal, and they gave me my silver medal. After, they quickly changed for a photo. They wore these white shirts reading Section 1 champions with the New York state outline on them. They held a banner reading out the same thing the shirts had, for a photo I would see everywhere. That night, I went to bed crying. I would never get to play high school volleyball again, and my dream of winning the section would never come true. As I closed my eyes, I held my baby blanket extra tight for a sense of comfort. The baby blanket is rectangular, its dimensions slightly uneven, with one side just a bit larger than the other. Its faded lavender scent still lingers faintly from the time last time I washed it. One side of the blanket has a light baby green fabric. Its fleecy texture is still soft to the touch, though the fibers have worn down a bit over the eighteen years of use. This side of the blanket has a small rough patch where the fabric feels uneven and slightly abrasive. It is not as soft as the rest of the fleecy material, with a noticeable contrast to the touch. The other side is a patchwork of vertical stripes, baby pink, yellow, green, and blue. All the colors, once vibrant, faded slightly as the years passed, with pink losing the most pigment. This side is rougher in texture than the green side. It carries more of a polyester feel, is slightly stiffer, and is less appealing to the touch. The many times of washing and drying this blanket have left marks with little pills of light green scattered across the rainbow side of the blanket. On the edges where the rainbow pattern meets the green fabric, the pastel green stitching has started to pull, and the edges have started to curl. The soft, fleecy green and the rougher, faded stripes bring me back to my childhood with a feeling of comfort and a reminder of countless memories.

annotations.text: Its faded lavender scent still lingers faintly from the time last time I washed it.
The soft, fleecy green and the rougher, faded stripes bring me back to my childhood with a feeling of comfort and a reminder of countless memories.

paragraphs.body: My dreams have changed over the years. When I was little, they started wanting to be a mermaid, then a marine biologist. As I got older, my dreams became more realistic about wanting to work in the medical field and what colleges I dreamed of going to. My dreams included sports like volleyball. I had dreams of winning tournaments with my club. I wanted to win states with my high school team. My dream was to play in college and reach the highest level I could achieve. Not all dreams come true, though. I did not become a mermaid and do not aspire to work as a marine biologist anymore. For volleyball, I did not achieve my dreams of championships with the teams I cared deeply about. But most importantly, I let my younger self down by not playing at the highest level I could. I chose not to play in college and went to a bigger university where I did not know if I could even make the club team. I let my younger self down, knowing I stopped playing the sport I loved so much. While dreams do not always turn out how you hope to, they motivated me to get where I am today.

A childhood is not just years one to eighteen. Childhood is not just a simpler period of life. Childhood is not just a developmental stage in a person's life. Childhood is not a period completely separate from adulthood. Childhood means the experiences I look back on and want to relive. Childhood was when I would play four-square with your class every day during recess. My childhood was collecting shells on the beach with my parents while on vacation. Childhood is when weekends were for playing Just-Dance with friends not doing homework. Childhood was when I played mermaids in the pool because I wanted to be one. Childhood is when I played with dolls, giving them the realities we wanted to live. Childhood is waking up Christmas morning not knowing what Santa would put under the tree. Childhood was when I played volleyball because I truly loved the sport.

annotations.comment: Nice definition paragraph! These details are excellent :)
annotations.context_left: to relive. Childhood was when I would play four-square with your class every day during recess.

paragraphs.body: Volleyball was a part of my childhood. I started playing in a rec league in sixth grade and played for seven years, letting it define much of my childhood. My life revolved around practices three times a week, early mornings, and the grueling, weekend-long tournaments that stretched into long, exhausting days. I allowed the coaches I had over the years to shape not only my game but my personality. For those seven years, my identity was unmistakably tied to volleyball. My friends and family knew me as "the volleyball player." It wasn't just a sport; it was a label, a definition of who I was in the eyes of others and in my own eyes.

annotations.text: Volleyball was a part of my childhood.
It wasn't just a sport; it was a label, a definition of who I was in the eyes of others and in my own eyes.
annotations.comment: Aha! Lots of friction, here. Great move to pull the essay together :)

paragraphs.body: Coming to Pitt, I made the decision not to continue playing volleyball, a choice that meant letting go of my childhood dream. At Pitt I have found a different identity from volleyball as a student. I have been able to explore a new side of myself that I never got to before. I have had the chance to explore new interests and form relationships based on who I am now, not who I was. Volleyball will always be a part of my past, but coming to Pitt has allowed me to shift my identity, giving me the opportunity to grow in different parts of my life I never got to before.

My baby blanket was not just a silly gift I have kept all these years. My blanket is my childhood, holding all the memories I can reflect on. It reminds me of all the dreams about who I wanted to be and how I achieved some of them. My blanket reminds me of all the memories I will reflect on that made me who I am today. This blanket reminds me of what I aspired to be growing up and how far I have come since then. My baby blank gives me a sense of comfort no matter where I go in life. My baby blanket will forever hold my childhood dreams but will now hold new memories as it follows me to Pitt.

annotations.comment: Great revision! This is a lovely essay. Great sensory details and a clear sense of the "large subject." I'm quite taken with how you transition between paragraphs and ideas, too. Excellent work :)
